coredata.dc:title: Facile and scalable preparation of carbon dots with Schiff base structures toward an efficient corrosion inhibitor
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Li, Xue-Qi
Xu, Peng
Feng, Gan-Lin
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been demonstrated to be a novel, green and effective corrosion inhibitor. Nevertheless, it still remains an enormous challenge to obtain CDs corrosion inhibitors by an energy-efficient and time-saving way. Meanwhile, there is no investigation to evaluate the inhibition effect of CDs with Schiff base structures. To surmount the barrier, a facile and scalable approach is elaborately designed to prepare CDs with Schiff base structures (Sc-CDs) by Schiff base reaction using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) as precursors at 50 °C for 2 h, and their corrosion inhibiting properties for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution are systematacially investigated by weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurement for the first time. Significantly, Sc-CDs with a long-term dispersion stability in HCl solution reveal a prominent inhibition efficiency of >96 % only at 200 mg/L incorporation. And according to the analyses of electrochemistry, adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology, the inhibition mechanism of Sc-CDs is rationally attributed to the protective film established by Sc-CDs chemical and physical adsorptions. This work not only provides a facile, scalable, energy-efficient and time-saving approach to synthesize CDs, but also firstly evidences significant inhibition capacities of CDs with Schiff base structures. These findings would dramatically stimulate the development of truly low-cost and high-efficiency CDs corrosion inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion inhibitor
Carbon dots
Schiff base
Scalable preparation
Inhibition mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (with a size distribution, inset) and (b) HRTEM images of Sc-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of Sc-CDs; (b) High-resolution XPS C1s, (c) O1s and (d) N1s spectra of Sc-CDs.

(a) Maximum PL spectra and (b) UV–vis spectra of 1 M HCl solutions with various contents of Sc-CDs; (c) Maximum PL spectra and (d) UV–vis spectra of various concentrations of Sc-CDs in 1 M HCl solutions before and after two-week standing.

Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl including different contents of Sc-CDs: (a) η values of Sc-CDs under various immersion time; Electrochemical characterization of (b) Nyquist plots, (c) Bode plots, (d) PDP curves; Langmuir adsorption isotherm according to (e) EIS and (f) PDP data.

3D morphologies and height profiles along blue surfaces of Q235 carbon steel samples in different states: (a) original state, and states after 96 h immersion in 1 M HCl (b) without and (c) with 200 mg/L Sc-CDs.

SEM images: (a) the original Q235 carbon steel surface, (b) the steel sample immersed in 1 M HCl without and (c) with 200 mg/L Sc-CDs for 96 h; The corresponding EDS spectra: the distribution of C and O elements from the steel surface immersed in 1 M HCl (d) without and (e) with 200 mg/L Sc-CDs for 96 h.
(a) XPS full spectrum and (b) High-resolution N1s spectrum of Q235 carbon steel surface protected by 200 mg/L Sc-CDs.
Inhibition mechanism of Sc-CDs for Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution.
Schematic of the preparation procedure for Sc-CDs.
The fitting parameters of Q235 carbon steel obtained from EIS results of 1 M HCl with various contents of Sc-CDs.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from PDP curves of 1 M HCl with various contents of Sc-CDs.

head.ce:title: Facile and scalable preparation of carbon dots with Schiff base structures toward an efficient corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, Guangdong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure
Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering
College of Civil and Transportation Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been demonstrated to be a novel, green and effective corrosion inhibitor. Nevertheless, it still remains an enormous challenge to obtain CDs corrosion inhibitors by an energy-efficient and time-saving way. Meanwhile, there is no investigation to evaluate the inhibition effect of CDs with Schiff base structures. To surmount the barrier, a facile and scalable approach is elaborately designed to prepare CDs with Schiff base structures (Sc-CDs) by Schiff base reaction using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) as precursors at 50 °C for 2 h, and their corrosion inhibiting properties for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution are systematacially investigated by weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurement for the first time. Significantly, Sc-CDs with a long-term dispersion stability in HCl solution reveal a prominent inhibition efficiency of >96 % only at 200 mg/L incorporation. And according to the analyses of electrochemistry, adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology, the inhibition mechanism of Sc-CDs is rationally attributed to the protective film established by Sc-CDs chemical and physical adsorptions. This work not only provides a facile, scalable, energy-efficient and time-saving approach to synthesize CDs, but also firstly evidences significant inhibition capacities of CDs with Schiff base structures. These findings would dramatically stimulate the development of truly low-cost and high-efficiency CDs corrosion inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion has brought multitudes of difficulties to the development of human civilization from the ancient time till now. It severely threatens the safety and stability of metallic equipment, dramatically exacerbates the environmental pollution and unavoidably causes substantial economic losses. An estimated number of more than $4 trillion per year around the world have been wasted by the direct metallic corrosion [1]. Therefore, how to effectively reduce metal corrosion becomes an urgent issue to be overcome. Up to now, a vast range of approaches have been developed, such as inhibitor, anticorrosive coating and cathode protection. Among them, inhibitors have attracted increasing interest and been extensively employed benefiting from their superiorities of significant effects, simple operation and high economic efficiency [2]. Unfortunately, although conventional organic inhibitors are able to significantly suppress metal corrosion, they still suffer from obvious drawbacks of environmental contamination and inferior solubility owing to their toxic elements and complex structure [3]. Hence, massive efforts have been made to develop environmentally friendly and highly water-soluble corrosion inhibitors. For instance, ionic liquids [4,5], plant extracts [2,3], and amino acid [6,7] functioning as green corrosion inhibitors have attracted increasing interest in recent years. However, they fail to be regarded as ideal green corrosion inhibitors, since they are subject to complicated preparation procedure (plant extracts) and high-cost raw materials (ionic liquids and amino acids), tremendously increasing their cost. Thus, the above factors vastly restrict their extensive application.

Exhilaratingly, on account of excellent water solubility, easily available precursors, diverse synthetic methods, ultrasmall size, high chemical stability and benign environmental friendliness, carbon dots (CDs) with prominent corrosion inhibition properties have been a promising candidate for the next generation green corrosion inhibitor [8–12]. CDs as a new zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial were serendipitously discovered in 2004 [13]. In general, CDs are defined as surface-passivated carbon nanoparticles with <10 nm in size and fluorescence as the instinct characteristic [14,15]. The application of CDs in the corrosion inhibition started rather late in comparison with their bioimaging [16], nanomedicine [17], optoelectronic devices [18], sensing [19], catalysis [20] and lubrication [21] applications. Until 2017, Zhao's group firstly demonstrated the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs. In detail, they prepared CDs via solvothermal treatment using aminosalicylic acid as the precursor, and employed the resultant CDs as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in HCl solution. Remarkably, the CDs revealed an excellent inhibition efficiency of >90 % only at 100 mg/L [9]. Inspired by it, Chen's group synthesized N, S co-doped CDs by hydrothermal treatment with aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as precursors, and adopted the CDs as efficient corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in NaCl solution [8] and aluminium alloy in HCl solution [10] respectively. Afterwards, Chen's group [22,23], Ye's group [24], Zhao's group [25], Yadav's group [26], Zhang's group [27,28], Liu's group [29], Cui's group [30] and He's group [31] unveiled the outstanding inhibition actions of imidazole-functionalized CDs, N-doped CDs and N, S co-doped CDs fabricated by solvothermal/hydrothermal procedure. And the above CDs at low incorporation even owned a high inhibition efficiency of >90 % for both steel and copper in HCl/H2SO4 solution. Noticeably, enormous progress has been made in the enhancement of CDs inhibition performance. However, owing to <60 literatures on corrosion inhibition application of CDs, the related research is still in an infancy stage, and there are still multitudes of issues. Among them, highly energy-intensive and time-consuming preparation process is the most exigent problem to be solved. All the above reports adopt hydrothermal/solvothermal approach to form CDs, giving rise to high energy and time consumption during the heating [32]. More importantly, it is difficult to achieve scalable preparation of CDs due to the complex manipulation. These above shortcomings inevitably result in the great increasing of the cost for CDs corrosion inhibitors, thereby immensely compromising their advantages. As a result, it is urgently required to exploit a facile and scalable route for energy-efficient and time-saving fabrication of CDs.

Recently, a novel preparation strategy based on Schiff base reaction was deliberately designed, and it furnished a green, mild, facile, energy-saving, large-scale preparation of CDs at low temperature [33–37]. For example, Gu's group utilized o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and catechol as precursors to obtained CDs at 80 °C for 3 h via Schiff base reaction and oxidation/polymerization [37]. Whereas, to the best of our knowledge, there are no literatures to investigate the inhibition performance of CDs with Schiff base structures until now. As is well-known, Schiff bases and their derivatives can serve as effective corrosion inhibitors by means of their high-efficiency adsorption on mental surfaces [38,39]. Furthermore, it has been manifested that N doping endows CDs more efficient inhibiting effects [8–10,22–31]. On the basis of the above analyses, there are considerable reasons to believe that CDs with Schiff base structures are expected to possess outstanding inhibiting behaviors. Therefore, it is highly necessary to collect CDs with Schiff base structures by elaborately optimizing the existing approach, and then explore their inhibiting properties.

In present work, a facile and scalable strategy is developed to prepare CDs with Schiff base structures (Sc-CDs) via Schiff base reaction using o-PD and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) as precursors at low temperature (50 °C) for 2 h (Scheme 1 ), and their corrosion inhibiting properties are systematacially investigated by weight loss test, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurement for the first time. Remarkably, numerous O- and N-containing surface functional groups confer Sc-CDs a long-term dispersion stability in HCl solution. On this premise, Sc-CDs only at 200 mg/L concentration present an outstanding inhibition rate of >96 % for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. In addition, their inhibition mechanism is rationally proposed in light of electrochemistry analyses, adsorption isotherm studies and corrosion morphology characterization. These findings would be of fundamental importance to promote the development of truly low-cost and high-efficiency CDs corrosion inhibitors.

The p-BQ (AR grade) and o-PD (AR grade) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Q235 carbon steel obtained from local suppliers was used for weight loss and electrochemical studies, containing the following elements (wt%): C 0.2, Si 0.3, Mn 1.20, Cr 0.25, P 0.045, S 0.05, Cu 0.25, Ni 0.25 and Fe remainder. Before each experiment, Q235 carbon steel specimens were polished using several grades of emery papers ranging from 400 to 2000 grid sequentially, then washed by deionized (DI) water and degreased with ethanol, finally dried under cold air flow. Concentrated HCl (AR grade, 36 % ~ 38 %) provided by Merck Millipore Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) was diluted to the content of 1 mol/L (1 M). DI water was used throughout the experiment.

Inspired by the literatures [36,37], the preparation method of Sc-CDs is deliberately designed as follows. Typically, 0.8 g p-BQ and 0.8 g o-PD were added into 160 mL DI water to form homogeneous mixture under ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, the mixed solution was continuously shaken at 50 °C for 2 h using a constant temperature incubator shaker. Then the resultant dark brown solution was purified by centrifugation and vacuum suction filtration (with a 0.2 μm membrane) and dialysis (with a 100–500 Da membrane) to remove residual sediments and unreacted substances. Finally, brown Sc-CDs in solid state were collected after rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed by a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA) operating at 200 kV. Raman spectra were measured using an XploRA spectrometer (Horiba, France) with a 633 nm excitation wavelength. Fourier transform infrwered (FTIR) spectra from 600 to 3600 cm−1 were taken on a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were confirmed by a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with an Al Kα 280 eV excitation source. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were carried out adopting a Lambda 750 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) with a 1.0 cm optical path. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette.

The Q235 carbon steel specimens with the dimension of 15 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm were immersed in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of diverse concentrations of Sc-CDs for 96 h, and were rapidly weighed three times after washing and drying at 24-h intervals. Before the test, all the specimens were weighted three times employing an electronic scale with a precision of 0.0001 g. The weight loss test was performed at room temperature. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency (η) are calculated following the equation below:
(1)
v corr
=
∆ W
A
t
(2)
η =
v corr
−
v corr ′
v corr
× 100 %
where v corr, ∆W, A and t represent the corrosion rate, the average weight loss, the surface area, the immersion time of Q235 carbon steel samples, respectively; v corr and v ' corr are associated with the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations of Sc-CDs, respectively.

The test solutions with the addition of Sc-CDs were prepared as follows: Sc-CDs were directly added to 1 M HCl solution to form different homogeneous HCl solutions with 25, 50, 100 and 200 mg/L content of Sc-CDs respectively under the glass rod stirring for several minutes. A PARSTAT 4000+ electrochemical station with a classical three-electrode system was employed for EIS and PDP measurement at room temperature. The Q235 carbon steel sample, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum plate were used as the working electrode (WE), the reference electrode (RE) and the counter electrode (CE), respectively. WE with the size of 15 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm and the exposure area of 1 cm2 was placed into a self-made container. Prior to test, open circuit potential (OCP) must reach a stable state. EIS was measured from 105 to 10−2 Hz with 10 mV amplitude sinusoidal voltage, and EIS data were fitted by ZSimDemo 3.30 software. The PDP curves were executed through scanning the potential range within ±250 mV versus OCP at a scanning rate of 1 mV s−1. All the electrochemical experiments were performed three times to ensure the reproducibility.

After the weight loss test, the steel specimen was washed, dried and stored in vacuum oven for corrosion morphology observation. Three-dimensional (3D) morphologies of all the sample surfaces were measured through a VR-3000 3D scanning measuring instrument (Keyence, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and elemental compositions of all the specimen surfaces were conducted on a Sigma 300 scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS) analyses. XPS for the chemical states of all the specimen surfaces were recorded using a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA).

In this study, Sc-CDs are prepared at 50 °C for 2 h only using p-BQ and o-PD as precursors without special oxidation reagents and equipment. The synthesis process is reasonably proposed as follows (Fig. 1 ). Firstly, o-PD dimer, o-PD polymer, and 2, 3-dinitro-phenazinemethane are formed through the oxidation/polymerization reactions of o-PD by reason of high activity of –NH2 groups from o-PD [36]. Then, Schiff base reaction occurs between CO groups of p-BQ and –NH2 groups of o-PD/o-PD oxidation products. Subsequently, via dehydration, condensation and polymerization, various polymer fragments are formed [37]. After that, aromatic clusters are generated by further carbonization and aromatization of these polymer fragments, eventually converting into Sc-CDs. Crucially, it is effortless to massively fabricate Sc-CDs only by proportionally increasing the quantity of raw materials. To sum up, the designed approach can accomplish the facile, scalable and low-cost preparation of CDs with Schiff base structures, avoiding the highly energy-intensive and time-consuming preparation protocol. It should be noted that owing to a relatively low degree of carbonization under the low temperature atmosphere, the as-obtained CDs tend to possess the unfavorable crystallinity, but it fails to deteriorate the corrosion inhibiting action of Sc-CDs.

The particle size distribution and morphology of the as-prepared Sc-CDs are directly characterized by TEM. Obviously, the monodispersed and quasi-spherical Sc-CDs are favorably distributed without aggregation (Fig. 2a), and have a size distribution of 2.56–10.84 nm with an average size of 7.06 nm, confirmed by randomly measuring >100 particles; While high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals the combination of segmental crystallization (with discernible lattice spacing of 0.24 nm coinciding with (1120) lattice fringes of graphene) and amorphous structures from the majority of Sc-CDs (Fig. 2b), suggesting a relatively low degree of carbonization. The result from the Raman scattering of Sc-CDs supports the above observation of HRTEM. As shown in Fig. S1, there are two evident peaks at 1374 and 1531 cm−1, ascribed to the D band (disordered carbon or defective carbon) and G band (sp2-hybridized carbon), respectively [36]. The intensity ratio of characteristic Raman bands (I D/I G) is 1.84, further implying a low degree of graphitization and multitudes of defects of Sc-CDs [36]. Overall, the morphology and structure analyses substantially evidence the successful formation and the rationality of the proposed synthesis process of Sc-CDs.

The optical properties of Sc-CDs are depicted in Fig. S3. The 1 mg/mL Sc-CDs aqueous solution displays faint yellow color when exposed to visible light and emits a bright orange PL excited by 365 nm UV light (Fig. S3a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3a) exhibits that Sc-CDs has two distinct absorption peaks at 293 and 370 nm, assigned to π − π* and n − π* transition of CC bond and nitrogen/oxygen-based groups respectively. This finding implies that unfavorable crystalline structure and abundant surface groups of Sc-CDs bring about two kinds of electron absorption states. As illustrated in PL spectra (Fig. S3b), Sc-CDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL behavior with the excitation/emission maximum at 430/576 nm. The above optical behaviors provide a unique route to assess the long-term dispersion stability of Sc-CDs.

In brief, all the characterization above reveal that o-PD and p-BQ undergo the Schiff base reaction, dehydration, condensation, polymerization and carbonization under low temperature to fabricate orange emissive Sc-CDs with unfavorable crystallinity, abundant O- and N-containing functional groups and Schiff base structures. These groups can confer high water solubility upon Sc-CDs, and Schiff base structures potentially contribute to make Sc-CDs serve as a promising corrosion inhibitor.

It is well known that good dispersion stability of corrosion inhibitors in corrosive solution is a crucial prerequisite for their practical application [41–43]. As shown in Fig. S4, after storing for two weeks at room temperature, there is no observable sediment in 1 M HCl solutions with different concentrations of Sc-CDs. When exposed to daylight, blank HCl solution shows colorlessness, while the color of the solution changes from faint yellow to brown yellow with Sc-CDs incorporation increasing. Under the excitation of UV light, it is evident that blank HCl solution fails to emit any PL; While after the addition of Sc-CDs, the solution emits red PL, and the red PL emission gradually enhances with the rising of Sc-CDs content. This phenomenon unveils that Sc-CDs are well dispersive without agglomeration, since their agglomeration tends to cause fluorescence quenching (namely aggregation-induced quenching effect) [21]. The above observations quantitatively confirm distinguished long-term dispersion stability of Sc-CDs in HCl solution. To accurately verify the long-term dispersion stability of Sc-CDs in HCl solution, their UV–vis and maximum PL spectra before and after two-week standing are recorded (Fig. 4 ). According to the curves in Fig. 4a, the intensity of emission maximum peak (around 624 nm) of the solution is in proportion to the concentration of Sc-CDs. Congruously, the UV–vis absorption peak (approximately 288 nm) of the solution shares the identical trend of PL peaks (Fig. 4b). Crucially, the peak intensities of all the solutions with different addition of Sc-CDs remain almost unchanged, regardless of UV–vis (Fig. 4c) and maximum PL spectra (Fig. 4d). These findings quantitatively demonstrate excellent long-term dispersion stability of Sc-CDs in HCl solution, precisely according with qualitative observation. It should be mentioned that with reference to those of Sc-CDs aqueous solution, PL and UV–vis spectra of Sc-CDs in 1 M HCl solution go through apparent variations on account of the great influence of protonation event [44,45]. In a word, all the qualitative and quantitative analyses simultaneously manifest the long-term dispersion stability of Sc-CDs in 1 M HCl, offering an essential precondition for Sc-CDs application as an acid-based corrosion inhibitor.

To get insight into effects of Sc-CDs as an inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl, the weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement are executed. Fig. S5 and Fig. 5a illustrate the corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel and inhibition efficiency (η) values of Sc-CDs in 1 M HCl after various immersion time at room temperature, respectively. The detailed parameters are tabulated in Table S1. In sharp contrast to that of the blank HCl solution, after addition of Sc-CDs, the corrosion rate vastly declines regardless of Sc-CDs concentration or immersion time. With the concentration of Sc-CDs increasing, the corrosion rate has a significant downtrend. And the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel attains a minimum value when Sc-CDs with 200 mg/L content after immersion of 24 h. Compared with that of blank HCl solution, the above corrosion rate holds an order of magnitude lower (from 3.73 to 0.37 g∙cm−2∙h−1) (Table S1). More importantly, in the presence of Sc-CDs, the corrosion rate reveals a downtrend with the extension of immersion time (from 24 to 96 h) (Fig. S5), implying the long-term corrosion inhibiting action of Sc-CDs. Identically, the highest η value is 90.03 % at 200 mg/L content of Sc-CDs after immersion of 24 h and increases to 93.53 % with prolonging immersion to 96 h (Fig. 5a and Table S1). The conclusion above obviously illustrates the eminent efficient inhibition performance and long-term corrosion inhibiting potential of Sc-CDs.

To further explicate inhibition properties of Sc-CDs, EIS and PDP of Q235 carbon steel as the WE in 1 M HCl solution with different concentrations of Sc-CDs at room temperature are measured. EIS is carried out to study the behavior of the carbon steel/solution interface. As described in Fig. 5b, the capacitive semicircles are observed from the typical Nyquist plots, and their depressed shapes are attributed to the surface inhomogeneity and roughness of the steel surface [30]. Clearly, their diameters enlarge with the incorporation of Sc-CDs, demonstrating that Sc-CDs efficiently retard the charge transfer process, thus suppressing the steel corrosion in HCl solution [30]. The inhibiting ability possibly originates from an efficacious protective film formed by Sc-CDs adsorption on the mental surface [46]. With the Sc-CDs concentration growing, the protective film is prone to be compact, and provides a more effective inhibition, eventually enlarging the diameter of the capacitive semicircle. Noteworthy, whether Sc-CDs exist or not, all capacitive semicircles show the similar shapes, deducing that Sc-CDs cannot change the electrochemical characteristics of steel electrode. As illustrated in Bode plots (Fig. 5c), the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (0.01 Hz) (namely |Z|0.01 Hz) possesses an inverse relation with the corrosion rate of the steel electrode. Distinctly, there is a rising trend for |Z|0.01 Hz values with the growth of Sc-CDs concentrations, reflecting a gradually enhanced corrosion inhibiting performance afforded by Sc-CDs for carbon steel. Significantly, the |Z|0.01 Hz value reaches 434 Ω cm2 in the presence of 200 mg/L Sc-CDs, improving 94.66 % than that of blank HCl solution. Moreover, all the Bode-phase plots present one single peak at the high and medium frequencies, denoting one time constant. Whereas their phase angle boost with the increasing of Sc-CDs content, deducing that the adsorption of CDs on the steel/solution interface brings about a stronger capacitive response [47]. To better comprehend the corrosion process, the equivalent circuit model (Fig. S6) is employed to fit the EIS data, and relevant parameters are listed in Table 1 . Among them, R
s and R
p represent the solution resistance and polarization resistance, respectively. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (γ) is calculated by the equation: γ = (R
p − R
p0) / R
p × 100 %, where R
p and R
p0 correspond to the polarization resistances in the absence and presence of the corrosion inhibitor, respectively. CPEdl as the constant phase element denotes a double-layer capacitance instead of an ideal capacitor owing to the inhomogeneity on the steel/solution interface [48]. CPEdl reflects the corrosion reaction area [49], and is calculated by the equation: CPEdl = Y 0(2πf max)n–1, where Y 0 and f
max are the magnitude of the CPEdl and frequency at the maximum value of impedance imaginary component, respectively; n value signifies a phase shift (−1 < n < 1). With the adding of Sc-CDs, R s values have a minor change, implying little influence of Sc-CDs on R s; Conversely, R
p values display a dramatical enlargement, and CPEdl values share an evident decline, manifesting that since the steel surface is potentially covered by an efficacious Sc-CDs-adsorbed protective film, the corrosion area is reduced and the polarization resistance is enlarged. These positive effects are heightened overtly with the augment of Sc-CDs dosage. Correspondingly, the γ value attains a maximum of 94.66 % in the presence of 200 mg/L Sc-CDs within the existing concentration range, higher than that of other N-doping CDs [30,50]. The finding evidences that CDs with Schiff base structures tend to provides a more effective protection for steel in corrosive acid solution.

PDP curves are conducted to monitor the process of the anodic and cathodic reactions, and appraise the inhibiting performance of Sc-CDs on these reactions. Fig. 5d exhibits PDP curves of Q235 carbon steel electrode in HCl solutions with diverse Sc-CDs concentrations, and relative parameters are summarized in Table 2 calculating the Tafel part of PDP curves by classical extrapolation. Among them, E corr, i corr, β c, β a and IE belong to corrosion potential, corrosion current density, cathode slope, anode slope and inhibition efficiency, respectively. IE value is acquired by the equation: IE = (i0 corr – i corr)/i0 corr×100 %, where i0 corr and i corr are the corrosion current density with and without the corrosion inhibitor, respectively [46,47]. As shown in Table 2, with the addition of Sc-CDs, both β c and β a values are inclined to shift toward smaller values, and i corr values decrease by at least one order of magnitude by comparison with that of blank HCl solution, indicating that Sc-CDs act as mixed type inhibitor, and demonstrate an obvious inhibiting effect on both cathodic and anodic reactions [11,12]. It should be noting that i corr value remarkably displays a downtrend as Sc-CDs concentrations grow. Correspondingly, the IE value representing the inhibition ability of Sc-CDs demonstrates a rising trend, and attains the maximum of 96.03 % when the content of Sc-CDs is 200 mg/L. These observations are in good agreement with weight loss test and EIS results.

All the conclusions drawn from weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement substantiate that compared with other N-doping CDs, Sc-CDs exhibit a more outstanding inhibition efficiency of >95 % (measured by PDP) for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. Crucially, Sc-CDs possess a promising long-term inhibition potential. The reason is potentially attributed to the efficacious protective film formed by Sc-CDs adsorption. In addition, with respect to plant extracts as inhibitors, Sc-CDs accomplish a similar inhibition efficiency at a much lower concentration [51–53]. And compared with other N-doping CDs [47,54,55], Sc-CDs demonstrate a more remarkable inhibiting effect owing to their Schiff base structures.

It is crucial to identify appropriate adsorption isotherms for better understanding the interaction mechanism between inhibitors and the mental surface [56]. According to the equation θ/(1–θ) = K ads∙C inh (θ represents the surface coverage, and is calculated by the equations θ = γ /100 and θ = IE/100 in EIS and PDP results shown in Tables 1 and 2 respectively; K ads and C inh denote adsorption equilibrium constant and inhibitor concentration, respectively), both the EIS and PDP results yield the best fit of Langmuir adsorption isotherm model, presenting an ideal linear correlation with R2 of 0.9997 and 0.9929, shown in Fig. 5e and f respectively. Generally, adsorption types can be classified into physical adsorption, chemical adsorption and both. The standard free energy (ΔG 0 ads) is employed to ascertain the adsorption type of corrosion inhibitors, which is obtained through the equation ΔG 0 ads = –RTIn(1000K ads) (T and R are the thermodynamic temperature of 298 K and the universal gas constant of 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, respectively) [57]. And the ΔG 0 ads value of more than −20 kJ−1 mol−1 symbolizes physical adsorption between inhibitors and metal surface, while the ΔG 0 ads value of less than −40 kJ−1 mol−1 suggests chemical adsorption realized by the coordination bond [57]. The ΔG 0 ads values of Sc-CDs from EIS and PDP data are −27.61 and − 27.30 kJ−1 mol−1, respectively. Hence, Sc-CDs attach to the Q235 carbon steel surface by both chemisorption and physisorption.

To better understand the inhibition mechanism of Sc-CDs, the surface morphology of Q235 carbon steel exposed in 1 M HCl solution without and with 200 mg/L Sc-CDs for 96 h, are analyzed by 3D scanning measuring instrument, SEM and EDS. 3D morphologies (Fig. 6 ) distinctly uncover that the surface roughness of Q235 carbon steel. Prior to the immersion in 1 M HCl, the original steel sample holds a highly smooth surface with a surface roughness (S a) value of 2.377 μm. After immersion, the steel surface is severely corroded with a great number of large pits resulting from aggressive acid attack, thus its S a value sharply increases to 10.630 μm; While after incorporation of Sc-CDs, the steel surface becomes smoother with negligible pits, leading to the reduction of S a value to 2.933 μm (72.4 % drop compared with that of blank HCl solution). Notably, this value is close to that of the original Q235 carbon steel, exhibiting the eminent corrosion inhibiting effect of Sc-CDs. Furthermore, the corresponding height profiles along blue surfaces show the height variation of all the steel surfaces. With Sc-CDs addition, the height difference of the steel surface is around 0.022 mm, much less than that of steel surface from blank HCl solution (0.070 mm). As a result, Sc-CDs effectively inhibit the corrosion of HCl on Q235 carbon steel.

Another evidence from SEM images further certifies the result above. As shown in Fig. 7a, only a few polishing-induced scratches are found on the original Q235 carbon steel surface. After immersed in 1 M HCl for 96 h, the steel surface is seriously damaged with a number of cracks and large pits, and accumulated corrosion products possibly containing α-FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 covering the surface appear to be loose (Fig. 7b), implying that the grave pitting corrosion occurs on the mental surface. In stark contrast, with the Sc-CDs adding, all the cracks and apparent corrosion products disappear, and the metal surface is much smoother and more homogeneous with unobtrusive pits, suggesting that the existence of an efficient protective film formed by adsorbed Sc-CDs remarkably suppresses the corrosion (Fig. 7c). Moreover, elemental compositions and chemical states of various metal surfaces are measured by EDS and XPS, respectively. All the surfaces of Q235 carbon steel samples are predominated by C, O and Fe elements (Table S2). According to Fig. 7d and e, in terms of carbon steel surface corroded by blank HCl solution, the C element shows an inhomogeneous distribution, attributed to the inevitable adsorption of CO2 and the organics from the atmosphere [50]; Meanwhile, a large deal of O element covers the mental surface. More specifically, compared with those of original carbon steel, O element content sharply increases from 6.13 % to 51.23 %, and Fe element content enormously reduces from 74.01 % to 23.66 % (Table S2). Thus, all the above observations prove that a great number of corrosion products are formed on the metal surface in the blank HCl solution resulting from serious corrosion reaction. Inversely, pertaining to the steel surface protected by 200 mg/L Sc-CDs, the C element distributes uniformly, indicating the C element originates from Sc-CDs instead of C pollution from air, thus a uniform and intact protective film is generated by Sc-CDs absorption; At the same time, the O element possesses a much more homogeneous distribution and a lower content (Fig. 7d and e). In detail, by comparison of those of blank HCl solution, Fe element content on the steel surface apparently increases (from 23.66 % to 37.83 %) in the presence of 200 mg/L Sc-CDs, accompanied by the substantial reduction of O atom content (from 51.23 % to 34.31 %). This powerfully further supports the fact the homogenous protective film established by Sc-CDs adsorption enormously restrains mental corrosion inflicted by HCl [58], thereby giving rise to the formation of few corrosion products on the mental surface. XPS data further provide persuasive evidence for the conclusion from EDS. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, N element from Sc-CDs can be observed on the XPS full spectrum of the steel surface immersed by HCl solution with 200 mg/mL Sc-CDs for 96 h, suggesting that Sc-CDs successfully adsorb on the steel surface to establish a protective film. Fig. 8b reveals three fitting peaks for the high resolution N1s spectrum: CN (398.48 eV), NFe (399.58 eV) and N+H– (400.58 eV). Among them, CN and N+H– (namely the protonation of nitrogen) stem from Sc-CDs; While NFe corroborates the presence of coordination bonds between N of Sc-CDs inhibitor and Fe of the steel surface [47]. Hence, the existence state of N on the steel surface suggests the existence of the protective film formed by Sc-CDs adsorption. Overall, all the corrosion morphology analyses firmly support the fact that Sc-CDs tend to adsorb onto the mental surface to form a homogenous protective film, thus efficiently inhibit the corrosion of HCl.

In light of all the analyses of electrochemistry, adsorption isotherm and corrosion morphology, a rational corrosion mechanism is established to illustrate the interaction between Sc-CDs and Q235 carbon steel surface (Fig. 9 ). The primary reaction of Q235 carbon steel in HCl without Sc-CDs is as follows: Fe – 2e− = Fe2+ for anodic reaction and 2H+ + 2e− = H2↑ for cathodic reaction, causing rapid dissolution of steel and H+ reduction, thereby generating severe corrosion on the metal surface. In the meantime, the steel surface is positively charged, thus hydrated chloride ions are prone to attach onto steel matrix surface through the electrostatic interaction [30]. While after incorporation of Sc-CDs, they are protonated immediately in 1 M HCl solution [6]. Subsequently, the attached hydrated chloride ions function as an interconnecting bridge to facilitate the physical adsorption of protonated Sc-CDs on the mental surface via electrostatic interaction, thereby occasioning a competitive relation with H+. According to adsorption isotherm investigation, apart from physical adsorption, chemical adsorption also plays a significant role. Benefiting from Sc-CDs abundant surface functional groups and Schiff base structures demonstrated by FTIR and XPS (Fig. 3), coordination bonds can be form through the donor-accepter interaction between the unoccupied 3d orbit of Fe atoms and the lone pair electrons of Sc-CDs O/N atoms, thus Sc-CDs achieve chemical absorption on the metal surface. Eventually, a protective film is successfully formed on carbon steel substrate by Sc-CDs chemical and physical adsorptions, testified by electrochemical characterization (Fig. 5), EDS (Fig. 7) and XPS (Fig. 8). With the assistance of the protective film, the direct contact between corrosive medium and mental can be effectively avoided, thereby simultaneously suppressing the hydrogen evolution process at cathode and metal dissolution process at anode (certified by PDP). In addition, with their content increasing, Sc-CDs can more efficiently adsorb onto the metal substrate to generate a more compact film, further mitigating corrosion of HCl (proved by EIS). To sum up, the protective film formed by Sc-CDs chemical and physical adsorptions on the metal matrix is responsible for their inhibition mechanism.

In summary, a facile and scalable strategy is elaborately developed to prepare Sc-CDs based on Schiff base reaction using o-PD and p-BQ as precursors at low temperature of 50 °C for 2 h, and their corrosion inhibiting properties are systematacially investigated by weight loss test, EIS and PDP measurement for the first time. Specifically, the proposed scheme avoids the highly energy-intensive and time-consuming process, and are capable of obtaining truly low-cost CDs with Schiff base structures. Sc-CDs possess unfavorable crystallinity but a great number of O- and N-containing functional groups and Schiff base structures. These groups endow Sc-CDs outstanding water solubility and considerable long-term dispersible stability in HCl solution. On this premise, Sc-CDs only at 200 mg/L concentration present an optimal inhibition efficiency of >96 % for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, thus it uncovers that CDs with Schiff base structures tend to own more significant corrosion inhibition. In addition, in consideration of electrochemistry analyses, adsorption isotherm investigation and corrosion morphology characterization, the inhibition mechanism of Sc-CDs is rationally attributed to the protectively film generated via Sc-CDs chemical and physical adsorptions. This work presents a facile and scalable synthetic technique for highly inhibition-efficiency CDs with Schiff base structures, thereby affording a novel opportunity for the green and sustainable development of low-cost CDs corrosion inhibitors.
Wu-Jian Long: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xue-Qi Li: Software, Writing – review & editing. Peng Xu: Investigation. Gan-Lin Feng: Data curation. Chuang He: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundations of China, NSFC-Shandong Joint Fund (No. U2006223), the Science and Technology Project of Shenzhen, China (No. JCYJ20190808151011502), the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Marine Civil Engineering (SZU) (No. 2020B1212060074) and Guangdong University Student Science and Technology Innovation Cultivation Special Fund Project (No. pdjh2022a0434). Furthermore, the authors would also like to thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS and TEM measurement. In addition, the authors thank Instrument Analysis Center of Shenzhen University for the assistance with TEM analyses.
